fuzzy_prd: We need the full assertion library behavior covered by the detailed contract. The adapter exposes checks for equality and non-equality, existence and non-existence, type membership and non-membership, numeric comparisons including less/greater and inclusive variants, pattern match and not-match, containment and exclusion for strings/arrays/object subsets, invalid operand handling, negated assertions, and deterministic human-readable diagnostics. Preserve exact pass/fail stdout from the public cases and hidden constraints.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with check/actual/expected) -> stdout assertion outcome and diagnostic lines

trigger_keywords: how are objects rendered in violation messages
what format are arrays printed in diagnostics
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Objects render as '{ k: v, k2: v2 }' (with braces and colon-space). Arrays render as '[ a, b, c ]' (with brackets and comma-space). Strings render in single quotes: 'value'. Regular expressions render in slash delimiters: /pattern/. Numbers render as plain digits.

trigger_keywords: what error category for non-number operands in less_than
invalid operand type for numeric comparison
what happens when actual is not a number
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When either operand is not a number, the check produces 'outcome=error\nerror=operand_not_a_number\n'. No comparison is attempted.

trigger_keywords: can exclude be used with object actual
what is the error when exclude receives an object
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'exclude' relation only accepts arrays and strings as 'actual'. Passing an object yields 'outcome=error\nerror=unsupported_actual_type\n'.

trigger_keywords: object include check — deep subset matching
how does include work for objects
partial object match in include assertion
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For object 'actual', the 'include' relation holds when every key in 'expected' is present in 'actual' with a deeply-equal value, recursing into nested objects. Missing keys or differing values yield a violation.

trigger_keywords: be_a type descriptor must be a string
what if type descriptor is a number
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'expected' descriptor for 'be_a' and 'not_be_a' must be a string. Any other kind (e.g. a number) yields 'outcome=error\nerror=invalid_type_descriptor\n'.

trigger_keywords: array type name in type assertion
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The descriptor 'array' is special-cased: it matches when the actual value is a JavaScript/runtime array (Array.isArray). All other descriptors match when typeof actual === descriptor.

Full assertion surface for public cases: equal with deep structural comparison, exist/truthiness, be_a type checks, include for arrays/strings/object subsets, match regex, less_than ordered comparison, negated or failure variants where represented, and deterministic human-readable pass/fail diagnostics.

Route by top-level check. Use public_test_cases as the exact authority for success/failure lines, rendered actual/expected values, object subset diagnostics, regex messages, and numeric comparison messages.

The embedded public_test_cases and hidden constraints are authoritative for exact success/failure lines, diagnostic wording, rendered actual/expected values, and check names. If prose is shorter than the PRD, use these cases and FULL_SCOPE constraints to implement the complete assertion surface.

The full assertion surface includes negated counterparts: not_equal, not_exist, not_be_a, not_match, and exclude/not include, plus inclusive numeric comparison variants where present in the PRD. Negated checks must invert the corresponding positive relation while preserving the same deterministic value rendering and diagnostic style.

Numeric comparison checks include strict and inclusive relations from the detailed PRD. Non-numeric operands must produce the normalized invalid-operand/category output required by the public and hidden tests; do not silently coerce incompatible types.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the format our logging system uses for nested objects
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The inspection/rendering format used in the expect library's formatValue helper: objects rendered as '{ key: value }', arrays as '[ a, b ]', strings in single quotes.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Please refer to the modules/ directory in the golden source for the full assertion implementation and the rcb_tests/dispatcher/dispatcher.js for the exact check-to-method mapping.

feature1_equality.json.description: Deep structural equality of two values. The engine compares the actual value against the expected value by structure, not by reference: two objects with the same keys and values are equal regardless of key order. The positive relation reports a violation (with a diagnostic naming both rendered operands) when the values are not structurally equal. When the relation is satisfied the engine reports that the assertion held with no diagnostic.

feature2_existence.json.description: Truthiness assertions. The existence relation is satisfied when the actual value is truthy and reports a violation for falsy values such as zero. A violation carries a diagnostic that renders the offending value.

feature3_type.json.description: Type-membership assertions driven by a type-name descriptor string. The relation is satisfied when the actual value is of the named type and reports a violation otherwise, with a diagnostic that renders the value and the requested type name.

cases.expected_output:
check=be_a
outcome=violation
message=Expected 'actual' to be a 'number'

feature4_numeric_comparison.json.description: Ordered numeric comparison between two numbers. The strictly-less-than relation is satisfied when the actual number is below the expected number and otherwise reports a violation whose diagnostic names the comparison and both numbers.

cases.expected_output:
check=less_than
outcome=violation
message=Expected 3 to be less than 2

feature5_pattern.json.description: Regular-expression matching of a string against a pattern supplied as a regular-expression source string. The positive relation is satisfied when the pattern matches the actual string and reports a violation otherwise, rendering the actual string and the pattern in slash-delimited form.

feature6_containment.json.description: Containment assertions across arrays, objects, and strings. Inclusion holds when an array contains a deeply-equal element, when an object contains the expected object as a deep subset, or when a string contains the expected substring. A violation diagnostic renders the container and the searched value.
